title: Wood Privacy Fence Installation

budget.description: Pressure-treated pine, 6ft standard privacy style, basic dog-ear pickets, $18-$27/linear ft for 150 linear feet

midRange.description: Cedar, 6ft, board-on-board or dog-ear style, $25-$35/linear ft for 150 linear feet

premium.description: Western red cedar or redwood, 8ft, shadowbox or horizontal plank design, custom gates, $40-$70/linear ft for 150 linear feet

costFactors.title: Wood Species
costFactors.description: Wood choice is the biggest material cost driver. Spruce and pressure-treated pine run $18-$27 per linear foot installed and last 15-20 years with proper maintenance. Cedar costs $25-$35 per linear foot and naturally resists rot for 20-30 years. Redwood is the premium option at $35-$50 per linear foot for standard grades, with premium heartwood redwood pushing $70-$120 per linear foot. For 150 linear feet, the difference between pine and cedar alone is $1,000-$1,200.

costFactors.title: Fence Height
costFactors.description: Standard 6ft privacy fences are the most common and cost-effective height. Stepping up to 8ft adds 25-35% to your total cost because you need longer posts, more pickets, and an additional horizontal rail. Some municipalities cap residential fence height at 6ft without a variance, so check local codes before planning an 8ft fence.

costFactors.title: Fence Style
costFactors.description: A basic flat-top privacy fence is the most affordable. Board-on-board (overlapping pickets) uses 30% more lumber but looks better from both sides. Shadowbox style alternates pickets for airflow and a finished look on both sides. Horizontal plank fences are trendy but require more precise carpentry and often cost 15-25% more than vertical styles.

costFactors.title: Terrain and Grading
costFactors.description: Flat, clear ground is the easiest and cheapest to fence. Slopes require either stepped panels (more posts, more labor) or racked panels that follow the grade. Rocky soil makes post-hole digging significantly harder. Tree roots, existing concrete, or utility lines can all add time and cost to the installation.

costFactors.title: Gates
costFactors.description: Each standard walk-through gate adds $200-$400 to the project. Double-wide gates for equipment or vehicle access run $400-$600. Custom gates with arched tops or decorative hardware push higher. Most residential fences need 1-2 gates, which adds $200-$1,200 to the total.

materialOptions.pros: Lowest cost, widely available, takes stain well
materialOptions.cons: Least durable, prone to warping, requires more frequent maintenance
materialOptions.bestFor: Tight budgets, temporary fencing, properties where appearance is secondary

materialOptions.pros: Affordable, rot-resistant treatment, 15-20 year lifespan with maintenance
materialOptions.cons: Green tint when new (fades), chemicals in treatment, can warp as it dries
materialOptions.bestFor: Best value for long-term durability on a budget

materialOptions.pros: Natural rot and insect resistance, beautiful grain, 20-30 year lifespan, no chemical treatment needed
materialOptions.cons: Costs more than pine, grays naturally without stain, softer wood can dent
materialOptions.bestFor: Most popular choice - great balance of appearance, durability, and cost

materialOptions.pros: Excellent natural rot resistance, strong, beautiful honey color
materialOptions.cons: Regional availability (best in Southeast), higher cost
materialOptions.bestFor: Southeast homeowners wanting a durable, attractive natural wood

materialOptions.pros: Premium appearance, exceptional durability, natural insect resistance, 30+ year lifespan
materialOptions.cons: Most expensive option, limited availability outside West Coast, sustainability concerns
materialOptions.bestFor: Premium builds where budget is less of a concern and aesthetics matter most

northeast.label: Northeast
northeast.note: Higher labor rates, cedar and redwood cost more due to shipping

westCoast.label: West Coast
westCoast.note: Highest labor costs, though redwood is more available locally

southeast.label: Southeast
southeast.note: Lower labor costs, cypress is affordable and locally available

midwest.label: Midwest
midwest.note: Competitive pricing, good cedar availability from northern suppliers

mountainWest.label: Mountain West
mountainWest.note: Moderate labor costs, growing construction demand

phases.name: Site preparation and old fence removal
Post hole digging and post setting

diyTasks: Digging post holes (rent a two-person auger)
Staining or sealing the finished fence
proOnlyTasks: Rocky or extremely difficult soil conditions
Fences on steep grades requiring custom engineering
Large projects over 300 linear feet where speed matters
diyVsPro.diyRisks: The most common DIY mistake is setting posts too shallow - posts should be buried at least 1/3 of their total length (2 feet for a 6ft fence). Improperly set posts will lean within a year. Also, hitting a utility line while digging can be dangerous and expensive - always call 811 before digging.

savingsTips: Choose pressure-treated pine over cedar. Pine lasts 15-20 years with a good stain and costs 20-30% less than cedar - saving $750-$1,200 on a typical 150 ft fence.

Set your own posts. Post-setting is the most labor-intensive part. Renting a two-person auger ($50-$100/day) and setting posts yourself saves $500-$1,000 even if you hire someone to install the rails and pickets.
Minimize the number of gates. Each gate adds $200-$600. Plan your layout to use as few gates as practical.
Buy materials directly from a lumberyard instead of a big-box store. Lumberyards often have better pricing on bulk fence materials and can cut boards to length.
Check if your neighbor will split the cost. Many states have shared fence laws, and even where they don't, neighbors often agree to split the cost of a fence on a property line.
Time your project for late fall or winter. Fence installers are less booked and may offer 10-15% lower rates during the off-season.
Stain the fence yourself after installation. Hiring a pro to stain adds $1-$3 per linear foot. A pump sprayer and a weekend of work saves $150-$450.
Use dog-ear pickets instead of flat-top. Dog-ear style costs the same but sheds water better, extending the life of your fence.

contractorQuestions.question: Will you call 811 for utility marking before digging?
contractorQuestions.why: Hitting a buried gas, water, or electric line is dangerous and expensive. Professional fence installers should always verify utility locations before digging post holes.

contractorQuestions.question: How deep will you set the posts, and will you use concrete?
contractorQuestions.why: Posts should be buried at least 2 feet for a 6ft fence, set in concrete. Some installers skip concrete to save time, but it significantly reduces fence longevity.

contractorQuestions.question: Does your quote include removing the old fence?
contractorQuestions.why: Old fence removal and disposal can add $3-$5 per linear foot. Make sure it's clear whether this is included or a separate charge.

contractorQuestions.question: What grade of lumber will you use?
contractorQuestions.why: There's a big quality range within each wood species. #1 grade cedar looks and performs much better than #2 or #3 grade. Make sure the quote specifies lumber grade.

contractorQuestions.question: How do you handle property line disputes?
contractorQuestions.why: A fence built even a few inches over the property line can become a legal headache. Professional installers should ask for a survey or set the fence 2-4 inches inside your property line.

contractorQuestions.question: Do you warranty your work against leaning posts?
contractorQuestions.why: Post failure is the most common fence problem. A good installer will warranty their post-setting work for at least 1-2 years.

contractorQuestions.question: What's your payment structure?
contractorQuestions.why: Avoid paying more than 30% upfront for materials. The balance should be due upon completion and your satisfaction with the work.

secondaryKeywords: how much does a fence cost
cost to install a wood fence
seo.metaDescription: How much does a wood privacy fence cost in 2026? National average is $4,500 for 150 linear feet. Get detailed cost breakdowns by wood type, regional pricing, and an interactive calculator.
